title: Why Does Losing $100 Hurt More Than Finding $100 Feels Good?
published_at: TODO -- fill in before publish -- ISO datetime, set on actual publish
updated_at: TODO -- fill in before publish -- ISO datetime
summary: Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky's prospect theory and loss aversion in behavioral economics and psychology, plus the 2018 challenge from David Gal and Derek Rucker and capuchin monkey experiments by Laurie Santos.
answer: TODO -- fill in before publish -- one paragraph direct answer; can adapt from script.md's cold open / closing reframe
duration_iso: TODO -- fill in before publish -- e.g. PT8M30S, fill in once the final render duration is known
key_findings: Later researchers tried to put an actual number on how much steeper.
This asymmetry doesn't stay confined to hypothetical gambling questions.
There's also a hint that this isn't purely a product of human economic culture.
There's also a neural signature for this, not just a behavioral one.

sources.title: Kahneman, D. & Tversky, A. (1979). Prospect Theory: An Analysis of Decision under Risk. Econometrica, 47(2), 263-291.
sources.publisher: Prospect theory -- reference-point-dependent evaluation; value function steeper for losses than gains

sources.title: The Sveriges Riksbank Prize in Economic Sciences in Memory of Alfred Nobel 2002 -- awarded to Daniel Kahneman (shared with Vernon L. Smith) "for having integrated insights from psychological research into economic science."
sources.publisher: Kahneman awarded 2002 Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences for prospect theory work; Tversky ineligible (died 1996, prize not awarded posthumously)

sources.title: Tversky, A. & Kahneman, D. (1992). Advances in Prospect Theory: Cumulative Representation of Uncertainty. Journal of Risk and Uncertainty, 5(4), 297-323.
sources.publisher: Loss-aversion coefficient (λ) estimates cluster 1.5-2.5, canonical ~2.25 value used in most textbook treatments

sources.title: Kahneman, D., Knetsch, J.L. & Thaler, R.H. (1991). Anomalies: The Endowment Effect, Loss Aversion, and Status Quo Bias. Journal of Economic Perspectives, 5(1), 193-206.
sources.publisher: Endowment effect, status quo bias, and related anomalies as downstream phenomena of loss aversion

sources.title: Chen, M.K., Lakshminarayanan, V. & Santos, L.R. (2006). How Basic Are Behavioral Biases? Evidence from Capuchin Monkey Trading Behavior. Journal of Political Economy, 114(3), 517-537.
sources.publisher: Capuchin monkeys show loss-averse trading behavior with token currency, suggesting the asymmetry predates human economic culture

sources.title: Gal, D. & Rucker, D.D. (2018). The Loss of Loss Aversion: Will It Loom Larger Than Its Gain? Journal of Consumer Psychology, 28(3), 497-516.
sources.publisher: Required hedge -- direct challenge to loss aversion's universality as a general psychological law, arguing current evidence doesn't support losses being more impactful than gains across the board

sources.title: Simonson, I. & Kivetz, R. (2018). Bringing (Contingent) Loss Aversion Down to Earth -- A Comment on Gal & Rucker's Rejection of "Losses Loom Larger Than Gains." Journal of Consumer Psychology, 28(3), 517-522.
sources.publisher: Response to Gal & Rucker -- pushed back on their overall conclusion but conceded loss aversion had been accepted too uncritically as a fixed, universal law rather than something context-dependent

sources.title: Yechiam, E. (2019). Acceptable Losses: The Debatable Origins of Loss Aversion. Psychological Research, 83(7), 1327-1339.
sources.publisher: Loss aversion effect is more reliable at larger stakes and becomes less consistent, sometimes absent, at smaller stakes -- directly relevant to the episode's own $100 example

sources.title: Tom, S.M., Fox, C.R., Trepel, C. & Poldrack, R.A. (2007). The Neural Basis of Loss Aversion in Decision-Making Under Risk. Science, 315(5811), 515-518.
sources.publisher: Neural basis of loss aversion: fMRI during 50/50 gambling decisions showed activity in gain-sensitive brain regions (including ventral striatum) increased with potential gain size but decreased with potential loss size; the steepness of this neural asymmetry individually predicted each participant's own behavioral loss aversion
